coredata.dc:description: As artificial intelligence (AI) technology evolves, data-driven approaches are gaining attention in predicting lithium-ion battery's remaining useful life (RUL). Indeed, accurate RUL prediction is challenging, primarily because of the complex nature of the work and dynamic shifts in model parameters. To address these challenges, this article comprehensively explores five significant publicly accessible lithium-ion battery datasets, encompassing diverse usage conditions and battery types, offering researchers a rich repository of experimental data. In particular, we not only provide detailed information and access addresses for each dataset, but also present, four innovative methods for battery aging health factor extraction. These methods, based on advanced AI techniques, are able to effectively identify and quantify key indicators of battery performance degradation, thereby enhancing the precision and dependability of RUL prediction. Additionally, the article identifies major challenges faced by current predictive techniques, including data quality, model generalization capabilities, and computational cost, highlighting the need for research focused on dataset diversity, multiple algorithm fusion, and hybrid physical-data-driven models to enhance prediction accuracy. We believe that this review will help researchers gain a comprehensive understanding of RUL estimation methods and promote the development of AI in battery.

head.ce:title: Advanced data-driven techniques in AI for predicting lithium-ion battery remaining useful life: a comprehensive review

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As artificial intelligence (AI) technology evolves, data-driven approaches are gaining attention in predicting lithium-ion battery's remaining useful life (RUL). Indeed, accurate RUL prediction is challenging, primarily because of the complex nature of the work and dynamic shifts in model parameters. To address these challenges, this article comprehensively explores five significant publicly accessible lithium-ion battery datasets, encompassing diverse usage conditions and battery types, offering researchers a rich repository of experimental data. In particular, we not only provide detailed information and access addresses for each dataset, but also present, four innovative methods for battery aging health factor extraction. These methods, based on advanced AI techniques, are able to effectively identify and quantify key indicators of battery performance degradation, thereby enhancing the precision and dependability of RUL prediction. Additionally, the article identifies major challenges faced by current predictive techniques, including data quality, model generalization capabilities, and computational cost, highlighting the need for research focused on dataset diversity, multiple algorithm fusion, and hybrid physical-data-driven models to enhance prediction accuracy. We believe that this review will help researchers gain a comprehensive understanding of RUL estimation methods and promote the development of AI in battery.

ce:section.ce:para: Under the background of the nation vigorously promoting the “dual-carbon” strategy, energy storage technology has been rapidly developed [1]. Lithium-ion batteries have obvious advantages in large-scale energy storage applications, thanks to their long cycle life, high energy density, and no environmental pollution, combined with mature energy storage system integration technology [2]. However, when lithium-ion batteries face complex operating environments with temperature fluctuations and different charging and discharging cycles, their electrochemical compositions undergo unfavorable changes, such as loss of lithium inventory (LLI), loss of active material (LAM), and rise in internal resistance, which is usually caused by internal mechanical stresses and complex side reactions triggered by a variety of external conditions, and ultimately result in a reduction in the capacity of the battery and a reduction in performance [3]. Fig. 1 displays a range of degradation mechanisms that are frequently observed in lithium-ion batteries. Specifically, LLI is mainly caused by side reactions that consume lithium ions, like the creation of a solid electrolyte interface (SEI), lithium deposition, and electrolyte decomposition [4]. Meanwhile, LAM usually originates from the chemical decomposition and dissolution of transition metals, which leads to the loss of active particles [5]. Conversely, the increase in internal resistance is primarily attributed to the formation of SEI films and the cracking of particles [6].

Generally, a battery is considered to have reached its end-of-life (EOL) once its state of health (SOH) declines to less than 80% of original capacity. Continuing to use a battery that exceeds the EOL may degrade system performance or even trigger a catastrophic safety event [7]. Therefore, accurately predicting a battery's remaining useful life (RUL) is essential for ensuring its reliability and security. Within a battery management system (BMS), RUL is a core state that estimates the remaining cycles or time until a battery reaches the end of its life cycle, based on its current condition. Accurate RUL prediction not only prevents risks due to battery aging, but also serves as a basis for developing battery maintenance strategies.

Currently, RUL prediction can be performed using model-based or data-driven methods. Typically, model-based methods usually rely on simplified physical models or empirical formulas with strong interpretability and reliability, but these methods are often difficult to capture the complexity and nonlinear characteristics of battery behavior, resulting in insufficient prediction accuracy [8]. In addition, model-based methods are less adaptable to changing operating conditions and aging processes, limiting their effectiveness in practical applications. To overcome these limitations, Zhang et al. [9] proposed a method combining expectation maximization, unscented particle filtering, and the Wilcoxon rank sum test, enhancing the model's adaptability to actual battery aging data through advanced filtering algorithms. Additionally, in a related study, Zhang et al. [10] predict battery RUL using a method that integrates the Kalman filter with expectation maximization and the Rauch–Tung–Striebel smoother. This approach is particularly effective for handling unlabeled small sample data and parameter uncertainty, significantly improving prediction accuracy. Over the past few years, as artificial intelligence (AI) technology continues to evolve, the field of battery performance evaluation has ushered in new methods and ideas. These AI methods rely on less physical background and reveal the degradation characteristics of batteries mainly by analyzing historical and actual operational data [11]. The core challenge of data-driven methods for predicting RUL is therefore how to use limited historical data to accurately reveal long-term degradation behavior over hundreds of charge and discharge cycles and thousands of hours of operation. Among them, techniques like machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) have advantages in modeling nonlinear systems and have been extensively studied for RUL prediction [12].

Although the abundance of review articles focusing on RUL prediction, only a select few provide a detailed overview of data-driven methods for estimating the battery RUL, particularly in electric vehicle (EV) applications. Such as the review article developed by Shao et al. [13] who used stochastic filtering techniques to elucidate the failure mechanisms of energy storage components, but did not go on to discuss data-driven methodologies in any depth or suggest where research needs to go in the future. Similarly, Zhao et al. [14] briefly explored data-driven and fusion approaches for RUL and state estimation within their review, but did not discuss key issues and challenges in detail. It also did not discuss future research directions. Hu et al. [15] evaluated multiple RUL prediction strategies, explored the challenges and future research directions, but did not address complex DL methods. In contrast, Wu et al. [16] reviewed RUL prediction models that employed data-driven methods, but only discussed in detail four problems related to lithium-ion battery RUL estimation. Overall, although most of these reviews focus on model-based and data-driven techniques for RUL prediction, a comprehensive survey that covers various RUL prediction methods and their principal challenges, and proposes viable directions for future research, still has not been fully developed yet.

This review aims to address the existing research gap by providing a detail analysis of RUL prediction techniques, which is an important addition to the existing literature. In particular, it emphasizes the issues and challenges in RUL prediction techniques and identifies future research directions. The principal contributions of this work are outlined as follows:
(1)
A comprehensive overview of currently widely used publicly available datasets and their various aging feature extraction methods are summarized, which contribute to a deeper understanding of the complex link between battery aging and SOH.
(2)
The development of AI methods in battery RUL prediction over the years is summarized, which shows how prediction accuracy can be improved by combining AI methods to process battery performance data, and strengths of AI applications within the field of battery technology.
(3)
The key issues and challenges faced by current AI methods in battery RUL prediction, such as battery data collection and quality issues, model generalization capabilities, and computational cost, are sorted out. Meanwhile, principal future research directions are pointed out with the aim of enhancing the precision of RUL forecast by fusing algorithms and enhancing the diversity and representativeness of battery datasets.

The structure of this paper is as follows: Section 2 introduces the available public datasets and explores the importance of these datasets in RUL prediction. Section 3 describes feature engineering techniques and compares the performance of different models while discussing the limitations of each algorithm. Section 4 summarizes the principal challenges facing the use of ML methods in RUL prediction. Section 5 explores future research directions in RUL prediction. Section 6 concludes the paper.

One of the cores of batteries from design, and modeling to management applications is battery data, but the current production of data demands substantial time and financial resources, which can span months or even years. Therefore, public datasets of battery testing in a standardized and easily accessible format have become a key resource to drive battery technology development, which not only facilitates practitioners' access to knowledge in related fields but also facilitates the validation and parsing of research results.

In this paper, we have collected five commonly used open datasets of lithium-ion battery laboratory tests, and briefly introduced the battery specifications and related experimental conditions of each dataset, as well as attached links to the data sources for downloading and reference use.
A.
NASA released two key public datasets on lithium-ion batteries in 2007 and 2009 that had a profound impact on the study of battery performance [17]. The first dataset used 18,650-type lithium-ion batteries rated at 2 Ah, while the second dataset used lithium-cobalt-oxide 18,650 batteries rated at 2.2 Ah. These datasets were cycle-tested at different ambient temperatures, both using a standard constant-current-constant-voltage (CC-CV) charging protocol and diverse discharge strategies. Initially, the battery charges in CC mode up to 4.2 V, after which it transitions to CV mode until the charge current declines to 20 mA. During discharge, the batteries are discharged at different nominal voltages to simulate the conditions of real-world use. In addition, the datasets include electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests of the batteries, where impedance data are obtained by scanning over a frequency range between 0.1 Hz and 5 kHz. Each dataset underwent up to seven sets of cycling tests at room temperature and 40 °C, respectively. In these tests, five sets of batteries were discharged using one of seven different discharge regimes after reaching 4.2 V, while the other two sets were subjected to random charging and discharging until the batteries' SOH declined to between 80% and 50%. These datasets detail current (I), voltage (V), and cell temperature (T), as well as discharge capacity (Q) and EIS data (IR), providing researchers around the world with invaluable experimental data for in-depth studies of the physics changes associated with the aging process and the subsequent decline in battery performance.
B.
The CALCE battery research team conducted exhaustive tests on five different sizes and shapes of batteries, INR 18650-20R, A123, CS2, CX2, and PL samples [18]. This section focuses on the data for two square batteries, CS2 and CX2. The charging cycle for both batteries followed the same criteria, i.e., charge at 0.5 C in constant current (CC) mode until reaching 4.2 V, then switch to constant voltage (CV) mode until the charging current declines below 0.05 A. Discharge conditions were categorized into six scenarios based on different discharge rates, depths, and ranges until the battery's SOH reached EOL. The test recordings included measurements of I, V, Q, and energy (E), as well as IR. Both the CS2 and CX2 batteries underwent the same charging process but with slight variations in the standard charging curves.
C.
Beyond the NASA and CALCE datasets, multiple available battery datasets from diverse origins are available. For example, a commercial lithium iron phosphate/graphite battery manufactured by A123 systems was used by the Toyota Research Institute, in collaboration with the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) and Stanford University, to investigate the impacts of rapid charging protocols on battery aging [19]. The batteries underwent cyclic charging under various rapid charging protocols until the batteries reached EOL at 30 °C. Data logging included battery T, I, V, Q, and IR. In addition, Sandia National Laboratories (SNL) tested three different types of 18,650 batteries, including A123 Systems' LFP, Panasonic's NCA, and LG Chem's NMC [20]. The tests considered different combinations of depth of discharge, ambient temperature, and discharge multiplier until the battery reached EOL. The recorded data included I, V, E, Q, and T. In contrast, the University of Oxford conducted a combination of aging cycle and calendar aging tests for its NCA/graphite battery for a period of three years to evaluate how the battery performs across various aging scenarios [21].

The NASA dataset, as the earliest publicly available dataset, laid the groundwork for subsequent research. The CALCE dataset encompasses various battery types and offers extensive experimental data for numerous applications. The MIT dataset focuses on how fast-charging technology impacts the lifespan of batteries. The SNL dataset emphasizes the importance of the depth of discharge in the battery cycle. The Oxford dataset provides long-term battery performance data under cyclic and calendar aging conditions. Table 1 gives basic information on these five datasets. Fig. 2 displays the degradation profiles of capacity for three of these datasets.

In summary, publicly available battery test data not just stimulate innovations in battery technology but provide a valuable reference for researchers to help them design more accurate and adaptable battery state estimation strategies for different application scenarios. Therefore, we encourage more organizations to share new, accessible datasets in a standardized format, so that we can promote the continuous progress of batteries and their management technologies.

Over the past few years, as AI has rapidly developed, data-driven RUL estimation methods have gradually attracted widespread attention. Compared with traditional model-based methods, the data-driven approach eliminates the need to investigate the battery's specific aging mechanisms, but it automatically maps inputs to outputs by optimizing the algorithm and using the dataset for training to achieve the prediction. The framework is illustrated in Fig. 3 . This section reviews prevalent lithium-ion battery RUL prediction methods. Comparing the architecture, functionality, and predictive outcomes of various approaches, presenting their strengths and weaknesses, and providing an objective evaluation of different RUL prediction techniques and strategies.

This method directly uses measured values like I, V, and T as inputs to the data-driven model and RUL as output. Although relevant studies have demonstrated the potential of this method in assessing the current SOH of a battery [23], there are still some limitations. First, it is not feasible to input all data directly into the model in practical situations. For example, due to the effects of aging, the number of samples taken from a battery during a charge/discharge cycle varies from as few as 800 sample points to as many as 5000 sample points. This inconsistency in the amount of data makes it difficult to use this data directly. Second, the raw data frequently fail to accurately depict battery aging, resulting in unsatisfactory predictive outcomes. Currently, such methods are still relatively limited in practical prediction applications, with only Zhang et al. [24] predicting battery RUL using raw EIS impedance data. This limitation may stem from the lack of available data: although each battery cell can provide data for hundreds of charge/discharge cycles for SOH prediction training, the life cycle of each cell only serves as an individual data point.

An alternative method is to train the model to predict capacity decay by learning to vary over short time intervals based on current capacity, I, and T [25]. The RUL can be forecasted based on the accumulation of capacity decay over these time intervals and observing when the resulting trajectory drops to the 80% norm.

In the methodology, health indicators (HIs) are initially derived from the available data through a preprocessing step. This not only helps to understand the aging of the battery from a physical point of view but is also important for assessing battery's SOH and RUL. Compared with purely data-driven methods that directly use raw data, the model constructed by feature-based methods is usually less complex, so it requires less data during training, is easier to implement, and is more suitable for RUL prediction. This method benefits from simplifying the model and its reliance on data by selecting features, thus improving the efficiency and applicability of the model. Currently, feature extraction techniques are categorized into four main methods. As shown in Fig. 4 , HIs are mainly extracted from the following four curves: voltage curves, time curves, increment capacity (IC) curves, and differential voltage (DV) curves. The following content will elaborate on the specific process of these HIs extraction.
(1)
HIs based on voltage curves [26]: As a battery is stored and used, its internal physical and chemical gradually change, leading to an increase in ohmic resistance and a loss of capacity. These changes usually cause significant variations in the voltage profile throughout the charging and discharging phases, making the voltage profile a critical foundation for assessing the battery health. During the charging process, such as voltage increase during equal charge time (VIECT), slope of charge voltage (SCV), upper voltage point (UVP), and initial charge voltage (ICV) can be used as HIs. Battery aging usually leads to an increase in VIECT and ICV and an advance in UVP. During the discharge process, voltage decrease during equal discharge time (VDEDT), initial discharge voltage (IDV), voltage distance (VD), and lower voltage point (LVP) can be used as indicators of battery health and reflect the impact of capacity reduction and increased internal resistance within the discharge phase.
(2)
HIs based on time curves [27]: Battery charging and discharging times are closely related to their rates, depths, and the extent of aging. In aging experiments, the rate and depth of charge/discharge usually remain constant, so the charge/discharge time decreases accordingly as the aging cycle increases. Time to equal voltage increase (TEVI) represents the duration needed to reach the preset voltage from the initial voltage, which decreases with aging. The time to equal voltage decrease (TEVD), which refers to the duration for the voltage to decline from an initial level to a predetermined level during discharge, and the time to equal current decrease (TECD), which refers to the duration for the voltage decline during CV charging, are both important time-based health indicators. In addition, the time for constant current charge (TCCC) and time for constant voltage charge (TCVC) directly reflect the capacity change during CC and CV charging. Time to equal temperature increase (TETI), measured from a temperature perspective, represents the duration needed to achieve a specific temperature starting from the initial temperature, and is an important time-based HI.
(3)
HIs based on IC curves [28]: IC analysis (ICA) serves as an effective, non-invasive method in electrochemistry, capable of detecting subtle shifts in electrochemical processes associated with capacity degradation. Unlike EIS or SEI measurements, voltage profiles, readily accessible from BMS, enable the derivation of IC curves. The key characteristic parameters of IC curves, like IC peak (ICP) and IC valley (ICV), IC peak location (ICPL) and IC valley location (ICVL), IC peak area (ICPA), and distance between two peaks (DBTP) are widely used as HIs. These parameters not just reflect the battery's aging condition but reveal more details about the internal electrochemical changes. For example, as capacity is lost, charge/discharge cycles are shortened, the voltage curve's slope intensifies, and the plateau rise is reduced, leading to lower ICP and ICV, and earlier ICPL and ICVL.
(4)
HIs based on DV curves [29]: The DV curve is commonly utilized in SOH estimation due to its reflection of electrochemical dynamics. Unlike the IC curve, the DV curve uses these peaks to signify the phase transition processes, and the valleys to indicate positions of phase equilibrium. The DV curve reveals the relationship between the DV value and the cell capacity. By extracting parameters such as DV peak (DVP), DV valley (DVV), DV peak location (DVPL), and DV valley location (DVVL), we can gain valuable information about the battery's aging state. For example, changes in DVP and DVV can indicate the cell phase transition process and shifts in equilibrium position. In addition, for cells with multiple valleys or peaks, the distance between two valleys (DBTV) is also used as a HI.

By extracting and analyzing these HIs above, the battery's health status during aging can be effectively assessed, and then RUL estimation can be performed. These methods have been widely researched and validated in practical applications and have become an important part of BMS.

Support vector machine (SVM) and its derivative algorithm support vector regression (SVR) are typical of ML methods. SVM is based on the principle of classifying the input data by constructing a separating hyperplane, with the goal of maximizing the geometric intervals in the training data set [30]. Similar to SVM, SVR constructs a hyperplane in a multidimensional feature space with the aim of minimizing the distance from the farthest sample point to the plane to build a linear regression model [31]. SVR offers benefits in handling small-sample data and time-series analysis, as well as fast computation speed, and thus is widely used in RUL prediction. However, the prediction effect of SVR depends largely on the kernel parameters, and there are difficulties in the selection of kernel parameters in practical situations, which often need to be paired with optimization methods to optimize the kernel parameters. For example, Zhang et al. [32] applied a SVR model optimized by the grey wolf optimization (GWO) algorithm to predict lithium-ion battery RUL, as depicted in Fig. 5 a. The optimization of the SVR kernel parameter through GWO significantly enhanced the prediction accuracy of RUL.

Gaussian process regression (GPR) [33] is a nonparametric model that applies the Gaussian process prior to regression analysis. Compared with neural network algorithms, GPR has been widely used in RUL forecasting. It demonstrates significant advantages in the prediction of time series data due to its simplicity of parameterization and ability to solve complex problems at low computational cost. Such as Pang et al. [34] developed a prediction model based on ICA and GPR, as shown in Fig. 5b, which not only provides an uncertainty representation of the prediction results but also demonstrates an excellent performance. However, the effectiveness of GPR largely depends on the choice of kernel function. For this reason, Wang et al. [35] presented a multi-kernel GPR model combined with adaptive feature fusion, using a method based on the drosophila optimization algorithm to optimize kernel function selection, which significantly improved the performance and robustness of the prediction.

The extreme learning machine (ELM) [36,37] is a novel single hidden layer feedforward neural network learning algorithm. Unlike traditional neural networks, ELM does not adhere to the gradient descent process. Instead, it randomly selects weights for the input layer and biases for the hidden layer. The weights of the output layer are determined by minimizing a loss function composed of the training error term and a regularization term for the output layer weights' norms. These weights are analytically derived using the Moore-Penrose generalized inverse matrix theory. Therefore, the ELM offers advantages such as fast training speed, fewer training parameters, and strong generalization ability, making it applicable in predicting the RUL of lithium-ion batteries. For example, Gu et al. [38] presented a prediction method based on transfer learning with full consideration of individual differences, as shown in Fig. 5c. Initially, a dual-stage ELM was utilized to construct a model capturing the relationship between capacity degradation rate and remaining capacity, thereby determining the regulation factor. Subsequently, an ELM-based transfer learning approach was employed to correlate the remaining capacity with RUL. Finally, the adjustment factor obtained in the first stage is used to adjust the prediction outcomes. This approach has better accuracy and efficiency. Gao et al. [39] proposed an improved grey wolf optimizer to optimize the deep extreme learning machine (CGWO-DELM) data-driven prediction approach. The CGWO optimized parameters such as the bias, input layer weight, activation function selection, and number of hidden layer nodes of the DELM, thus improving the accuracy of RUL prediction.

The DL efficiently handles supervised classification, modeling, or reinforcement learning problems by constructing multi-layer neural networks. As neural networks have automatic feature extraction capability and good generalization performance make they show obvious advantages in battery state estimation [40]. In the specific context of battery RUL prediction, prevalent data-driven techniques encompass convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and recurrent neural networks (RNNs).

CNN is widely utilized in computer vision and is also suitable for feature extraction of sequential data. Its structure contains local sensing fields, weight sharing, and pooling layers [41]. In practice, CNNs are usually used as the first layer of neural networks, utilizing convolutional kernels for multilevel feature extraction from local to global. Such as Hong et al. [42] utilized an extended CNN model to capture long-term dependencies in time series data to enhance the prediction accuracy and performance of RUL, as shown in Fig. 6 a. Although convolutional models enable parallel computation, fully convolutional sequence models face challenges due to their high memory demand, extensive parameters, and intricate tuning requirements. These factors contribute to difficulties in managing long sequence data, prolong training times for complex models, and limit scalability.

In contrast, RNN has significant advantages in processing sequence information and is particularly suitable for battery RUL prediction because of the significant temporal correlation of sequence data collected from batteries. However, traditional RNNs frequently encounter issues with exploding or vanishing gradients. To address this issue, long short-term memory (LSTM) neural network and gated recurrent unit (GRU) have been proposed. LSTM is able to efficiently manage long-term and short-term information in cellular and hidden states respectively through the mechanism of the forgetting gate and memory gate, thus realizing the persistent retention of important information [43]. The structure is shown in Fig. 6b. GRU, which evolved from LSTM networks, simplifies the structure by merging gating to achieve similar functionality and has been widely used as an alternative to LSTM for RUL prediction in recent years due to its simpler structure and reduced computational effort [44,45]. For example, Wang et al. [46] proposed an improved anti-noise adaptive long short-term memory (ANA-LSTM) neural network, which combined with an adaptive feedback correction strategy, to achieve high-precision RUL prediction through the cooperative construction of multiple features. Rastegarpanah et al. [47] used CNN and LSTM to estimate the RUL and improve the accuracy of prediction. In addition, Fan et al. [48] developed a predictive framework for RUL that integrates degradation features, including the knee point, as shown in Fig. 6c. This framework employs a GRU model to extract deep features from the relaxed voltage sequence and achieves joint prediction of the inflection point and RUL.

Although RNN is very suitable for RUL estimation, its recursive structure is difficult to parallelize and the information of long-term dependencies is easy to forget. Therefore, Bahdanau et al. [49] introduced the attention mechanism (AM), which can capture long-term dependencies in a sequence and focus attention on key information, thus overcoming these limitations of RNN. AM is processed with equal distances to all inputs in the sequence, avoiding loss of information, and is easy to process in parallel. This mechanism has been widely used in natural language processing and is gradually demonstrating its potential in the area of RUL assessment. For example, Chen et al. [50] presented the attention with mixture of experts (AttMoE) network, which integrates attention and mixture of expert systems, the structure is shown in Fig. 6d. This network optimizes the extraction and integration of battery capacity features, significantly enhancing prediction accuracy. Meanwhile, the CNN model based on AM and location coding developed by Zhou et al. [51] not only enhances the robustness of the network but also improves its performance.

Building on the success of the AM, the field of DL has further developed the transformer model, which is based entirely on the AM. The model employs an encoder and decoder architecture to explore the global dependencies between inputs and outputs [52,53]. The transformer is not only designed to support efficient parallel processing but also the length of the input-output sequences it processes need not be the same, a feature that makes it particularly suitable for RUL prediction. For example, Han et al. [54] presented a new denoising transformer-based neural network (DTNN) model, which provides insight into the behavior of lithium-ion batteries by filtering and capturing meaningful local data features through CNNs and then performs RUL prediction through the transformer model, which improves the prediction accuracy of the model. Furthermore, Zhao et al. [55] extracted key aging features from a large amount of raw data by combining feature engineering with the Transformer model and successfully predicted the battery's service life across different environments, as shown in Fig. 6e. Although transformer model shows advantages in RUL prediction, its applications in this field are still relatively few, and relevant research should be strengthened in the future.

This paper provides a detailed overview of the current research on data-driven RUL prediction methods for lithium-ion batteries, detailing established techniques and presenting their outcomes are shown in Table 2 .

While AI techniques show great potential for battery RUL prediction, in practice we must face several key technical challenges: data collection and quality, generalization capability of models, and computational cost. These challenges are illustrated in Fig. 7 .

The effectiveness and precision of data-driven approaches for predicting RUL are greatly dependent on data collection and quality. For instance, delays in collecting battery data hinder real-time access and can cause timing issues. This is especially true for battery data near the EOL, where the high degree of non-linearity makes data acquisition and processing more challenging. Secondly, the quality of the data collected can be significantly degraded due to differences between battery cells, less accurate sensors, and extreme charge and discharge rates [63]. In addition, the complex mechanical demands and varied properties of materials in lithium-ion batteries, including porosity, electrolytes, and diaphragms, further exacerbate these challenges. It is well known that high-quality data depends on three main factors: accuracy, abundance, and diversity [64]. For instance, limited data can diminish algorithm accuracy, while excessive data may lead to huge computational overheads and overfitting problems. Variations in environments along with the intricate nature of battery aging processes also hinder technological advancement and development. Gathering extensive data over extended periods proves to be both challenging and time-intensive. In addition, the volume of battery aging datasets can vary due to differences in V, C, T, and Q values. Therefore, to overcome the problems associated with data quality and quantity, battery parameters and data samples should be appropriately selected to optimize the battery dataset.

In the process of predicting RUL using a data-driven approach, the generalization ability of the model is crucial. The generalization ability refers to the model's prediction ability on unknown data, which directly affects the reliability and usefulness of the prediction outcomes. However, the model's generalization ability is easily limited by a variety of factors such as overfitting, limitations in feature extraction, inconsistency in data distribution, and lack of cross-domain data [65]. The phenomenon of overfitting commonly occurs when a model overlearns a specific pattern on a limited training dataset, causing it to perform poorly on new, unseen data. In addition, battery RUL prediction models rely on efficient feature extraction, but existing features may not adequately reflect the complexity of battery aging, especially when batteries are in different operating environments and usage conditions. The trend of battery performance degradation may vary over time and usage conditions, and this dynamic change in data distribution makes it difficult for the model to maintain good generalization performance in all cases. Meanwhile, due to the diversity of battery usage scenarios, while the training data are usually limited to specific environments and conditions, this further limits the model's generalization ability within different application scenarios. Several strategies can be employed to enhance the model's generalization ability. For example, model regularization and simplified model structure can be introduced to address overfitting risks. Cross-domain learning and migration learning can also be used to improve the model's generalization performance on different datasets. Additionally, the diversity of training data can be increased through data augmentation and synthesis to enable the model to adjust to the dynamic changes within data distribution [66].

As the model complexity increases and the amount of data grows, so do the computational resources required. Complex DL models often require multiple iterations for training, resulting in significantly longer computational cycles. This not only takes up a large number of computational resources but may also delay the deployment of the model. Meanwhile, the development of sensor technology and the increasing frequency and accuracy of battery data collection have generated massive amounts of data. Processing these large-scale datasets requires high-performance computational hardware and large-capacity storage facilities, which significantly increases the cost [67]. In real-time applications of BMS, the speed of model inference is critical. However, complex models often lead to high inference latency, requiring a trade-off between model accuracy and real-time performance. Moreover, computationally intensive tasks not only consume a large number of computational resources but also generate significant energy consumption, which is contrary to the current pursuit of energy saving and emission reduction goals. Meanwhile, energy-intensive computational activities put pressure on the environment, which is not in line with the green trend. To address these challenges, the adoption of multiple strategies is necessary. Techniques for model optimization and compression, including pruning, quantization, and knowledge distillation, can decrease the size and complexity of models while enhancing computational efficiency. The use of cloud computing and distributed computing resources can spread the computational load and reduce the computational cost at a single point. In addition, the development of efficient algorithms and computational frameworks, such as the use of GPU-accelerated computation, can reduce computation time and energy consumption. Effective data management and analysis strategies, such as the application of data compression and indexing techniques, can also optimize the data storage and retrieval process and reduce the time and cost of data processing [68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Diversity and representativeness of the dataset
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned in Section 4, the effectiveness of the AI algorithm is significantly influenced by both the volume and quality of the data provided. However, collecting large and diverse datasets is still a time-consuming and laborious task since experimental data are usually collected at a sampling frequency of 1 Hz [69–71]. However, different EV drive cycles produce distinct V and I value profiles, requiring multiple repetitions to accurately obtain the required data. While large datasets can improve the model accuracy during training, they also introduce computational burdens and potential overfitting problems. Therefore, in future research in RUL prediction, the focus should be on enhancing the diversity and representativeness of data to ensure the precision and dependability of forecasting models. Central to this strategy is the collection of data from a wide range of operating conditions so that the model can provide accurate RUL predictions across different usage environments and conditions. Diversification of data collection strategies is key to ensuring model adaptability. By simulating and recording battery performance data under different temperatures, charging and discharging rates, number of cycles, etc., both in the laboratory and in practical scenarios, it is possible to capture the performance of batteries in different environments, thereby enhancing the model's predictive capability. In addition, the use of advanced sensor technology to monitor key parameters such as V, I, T, etc. of the battery in real-time under dynamic operating conditions can provide more detailed and high-quality data. Enhancement of data representation is another key aspect, collecting data on lithium-ion batteries with different chemical compositions, structural designs, and manufacturing processes to cover a wider range of battery products. With such diverse data sources, our model will be able to reflect the performance characteristics of various kinds of batteries more comprehensively and improve the generalization ability of RUL estimation. Finally, there is also a need to consider privacy protection issues during data collection and storage. For example, it is possible to infer the owner's daily habits from the electric power usage data of an EV. To effectively address this issue, we advocate the development of effective data anonymization and aggregation strategies. These strategies not only protect user privacy but also ensure that key insights required for battery RUL prediction are not lost in the de-identification process. With this approach, the predictive accuracy and utility of the model can be maintained even under a strict privacy-preserving framework.

Currently, there are numerous methods for data-driven prediction of RUL, but there is no single data-driven approach is universally superior or widely accepted. When employing a specific method, the prediction performance is often limited, so fusion methods have received attention. Fusion algorithms are used to integrate multiple RUL prediction algorithms in a certain way, integrating the strengths of various models and mitigating the limitations of individual models, to achieve better prediction results. This type of method has gradually become an important development direction for RUL prediction in the future. Under AI technology, data-driven fusion methods are primarily categorized into two types: the integration of filtering algorithms and data-driven models, and the combination of various data-driven algorithms. These fused models capitalize on the strengths of individual algorithms while simultaneously processing extensive data and enhancing predictive accuracy.

In the integration of filtering algorithms with ML algorithms, Li et al. [72] constructed a battery health prediction system combining the GPR model and particle filtering (PF). In this system, the GPR model was used to analyze the statistical features of the battery degradation curve, while the PF focused on battery life prediction. Fang et al. [73] integrated SVR to enhance the prediction accuracy by relocating the particles through the PF. Chang et al. [74] presented a PF-based Relevance Vector Machine (RVM) model for predicting RUL, with special emphasis on calculating the uncertainty of the prediction via degenerate curves. Although system reliability is improved and maintenance costs are reduced, these curves obtained from non-dynamic operation restrict the broad deployment of the model. For the presence of Gaussian noise in linear systems, Kalman filtering (KF) techniques can be used as an alternative to PF. Song et al. [75] developed a fusion model of RVM and KF, which optimizes the estimates generated by RVM through KF. Xue et al. [76] constructed an SVR model combining adaptive unscented Kalman filtering (AUKF) and genetic algorithm (GA) optimization, which adaptively handles the noise covariance and observation noise covariance (AUKF-GA-SVR). Fig. 8 a depicts the flowchart of this approach. Zheng et al. [77] used Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) and Related Vector Regression (RVR) methods for short-term battery capacity prediction, and continuously updated the prediction model with the prediction information provided by RVR. Although some results were achieved, future research should further explore long-term capacity prediction.

Moreover, approaches that fuse multiple data-driven models with intelligent algorithms have attracted extensive attention from researchers. For example, Wang et al. [78] proposed an SVR technique combining differential evolution (DE) algorithm for RUL prediction. This method optimizes the kernel selection of the SVR model by DE, and its validation is based on previously published studies of fusion methods. Zhao et al. [79] presented a fusion RUL prediction approach based on deep belief network (DBN) and RVM, whereby DBN is responsible for extracting the decaying features of the capacity, and RVM provides RUL predictions by using the extracted features as inputs. While in the field of DL, Wang et al. [80] proposed an improved robust multi-timescale singular filtering-Gaussian process regression-long short-term memory (SF-GPR-LSTM) modeling method, which accurately estimates the multi-timescale state parameters under the influence of noise and nonlinear current variations through data fusion and correction, and realizes the residual capacity estimation of lithium-ion batteries at extreme low temperature. Chen et al. [81] presented a data-driven RUL prediction approach combining a grey neural network with a sliding-window grey model and a PF (GNN-SGMPF). This method significantly improves the prediction performance through new HIs and tracking degradation trends. Fig. 8b depicts the flowchart of this approach. Zhang et al. [82] presented a data-driven RUL prediction approach combining PF, temporal self-attention mechanism, and bi-directionally gated recurrent unit (BiGRU). This method significantly improves the prediction performance by giving specific meaning to the battery capacity at different time points through offline training on historical data. In the same vein, Sun et al. [83] developed an integrated RUL method incorporating improved variational modal decomposition (VMD), LSTM, and GPR. This method first decomposes the measured capacity dataset into residual and capacity regeneration components using the VMD algorithm, and then predicts them by LSTM and GPR models respectively and obtains the final battery capacity prediction by integrating the results of each prediction, which significantly enhances the precision of forecasts. Although the fusion approach yields improved outcomes over a single model structure, it also raises the model's computational complexity, and future research needs to explore effective strategies to reduce the computational complexity.
In order to clearly show the research of hybrid methods, the above methods are summarized and generalized, and the results are shown in Table 3 .

As can be seen from the above review of the various fusion methods, the accuracy of each method is based on specific considerations. In general, the key factors affecting the model performance usually include the type of model parameters, the number of samples extracted, the amount of data used to train the model, and the choice of hyperparameters. Furthermore, the accuracy of the data-driven method based on RUL prediction can be further improved by applying filtering techniques such as PF and KF. In this process, the use of intelligent algorithms for selecting appropriate data samples, tuning the model, and optimizing the hyperparameters can also greatly enhance the model's predictive accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fusion of mechanistic models and data-driven models
ce:section.ce:para: Physically based degradation models are primarily used to simulate the physical mechanisms of battery aging, with the aim of constructing models that can predict the future aging process of a battery. Ideally, if these aging mechanisms can be perfectly simulated, the model will be able to accurately predict the lifetime of the battery. However, in practice, these physically-based models are usually only validated under specific operating conditions and the generalization ability of the models appears limited when applied to unknown conditions. This phenomenon suggests that combining mechanistic models with data-driven approaches not only compensates for the lack of generalization of physical models but also enhances the predictive ability of the models, enabling them to more accurately predict aging differences between different batteries and multiple possible aging mechanisms.

Several promising frameworks have emerged, such as field inversion and machine learning paradigm [84], physics-informed neural networks (PINN) [85], neural ordinary differential equations [86], universal differential equations [87], and neural differential equations [88]. For example, Zhang et al. [89] used an equivalent circuit model to infer battery capacity from non-constant current data and parameterize the capacity decay curve. In addition, there are some other types of hybrid models, Wang et al. [90] proposed a PINN that achieves accurate prediction of battery SOH and RUL by combining empirical degradation and state space equations to simulate the attributes affecting battery degradation, and then capturing the battery degradation dynamics using the neural network. The method demonstrated significant performance in conventional, small sample, and transfer experiments compared to other neural networks. Fig. 9 a depicts the flowchart of this approach. The physics-based machine learning approach developed by Ma et al. [91] describes degradation-induced changes in the charging curve by building a model containing physical parameters, identifying the parameters from operational data using a nonlinear least squares (NLS) method, and mapping the aging-related parameters to the battery's SOH and RUL using a deep neural network (DNN) to map aging-related parameters to RUL, revealing the intrinsic connection between the model parameters and the battery degradation mechanism, and demonstrating accuracy, efficiency, and robustness beyond the existing methods. Fig. 9b depicts the flowchart of this approach (NLS-DNN). Jia et al. [92] proposed highly adaptive features reflecting the accelerated aging mechanism for accelerated aging diagnostics of lithium-ion batteries by introducing an integrated framework of aging mechanisms and data-driven methods. Han et al. [93] established an improved semi-empirical capacity decay model for lithium-ion batteries with full consideration of IR and T. The dataset of lithium-ion batteries was denoised by wavelet packets, then the parameters of the model were identified according to a GA, and a PF framework was designed to update the parameters of the model online. By integrating both aspects, the SOH and RUL of lithium-ion batteries can be predicted with high accuracy. Moreover, a hybrid model that combines physical mechanisms with data-driven methods can leverage the strengths of both data and model-based approaches, enhancing prediction precision and the model's explanatory power. This integration represents a crucial future direction for developing RUL prediction models for lithium-ion batteries.

This review details the application of AI techniques in predicting the RUL of lithium-ion batteries, covering the entire process from dataset acquisition, extraction of aging features, and model selection, to final RUL prediction. Initially, the article introduces and analyses five publicly available lithium-ion battery datasets from current authorities, highlights their importance for the development in this field, and encourages more organizations to share new, accessible, and formatted standardized datasets to jointly promote the continuous progress of batteries and their management technologies. It then delineates the procedure of RUL prediction utilizing data-driven methods. These approaches are categorized into two types: pure data approaches and feature-based approaches. Four different HIs extraction methods in the feature-based method are described in detail, and it is pointed out that the feature-based method not only helps to understand the battery's aging level from a physical perspective but also has great significance in assessing the SOH and RUL. Finally, the article provides a comprehensive assessment of various data-driven approaches for RUL prediction. The analysis considers various aspects such as method classification, application contribution, strengths, and weaknesses. Finally, it highlights the main key issues faced at present, including many challenges such as data collection and quality, model generalization capability, computational cost, etc. To overcome these problems, it is pointed out that next steps in research should make intensive efforts to enhance the diversity and representativeness of datasets, develop multiple algorithm fusion RUL prediction methods, and construct composite models that integrate physical mechanisms and data-driven techniques with strong generalization capabilities.

Through in-depth analyses and discussions, the article highlights the necessity of integrating multiple data sources and algorithms. The “No Free Lunch” theorem posits that no singular method is capable of addressing every problem. Therefore, different techniques and methods need to be applied flexibly according to specific situations. This article seeks to offer a thorough and detailed view, enhancing comprehension of existing research trends and prospective developments. With the continuous advancement and application broadening of AI technology, it is expected that more innovations and breakthroughs will be ushered in the area of battery longevity forecasting, providing solid scientific and technical support for the safety and reliability of global energy storage solutions.
Data will be made available on request.
